LAW AND MOTION, 302, HEARING RE: DEFENDANT WELLS FARGO BANK, N.A.'S MOTION TO TRANSFER. MATTER CONTESTED, ARGUED, AND THE COURT ADOPTED THE TENTATIVE RULING WITH THE MODIFICATION THAT THE MOTION WAS FILED BY DEFENDANT WELLS FARGO BANK ONLY. THE TENTATIVE IS ADOPTED AS FOLLOWS: DEFENDANT WELLS FARGO BANK'S MOTION TO TRANSFER VENUE IS DENIED. MR. OLSEN IS TO PREPARE THE PROPOSED FORM OF ORDER. JUDGE: ROCHELLE C. EAST. CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Jun-27-2024 Continued To Aug-21-24 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

MOTION TO TRANSFER
Off Calendar Jun-14-2024 Denied
MINI MINUTES: Appearances via Zoom:
Aaron Olsen, Esq. of, Tel. 619.400.7162, appearing for plaintiff Darshana Kamdar;
Stanley G. Stringfellow II, Tel. 949.450.4424, appearing for defendant Wells Fargo Bank.

Hearing re: DEFENDANT WELLS FARGO BANK, N.A.'S MOTION TO TRANSFER.

For clarification purposes, Mr. Stringfellow states that the motion before the court today was filed by defendant Wells Fargo Bank only.

The matter is contested. Argument is held. At the conclusion of argument, the Court adopted the tentative ruling with the modification that the motion was filed by defendant Wells Fargo Bank only as follows:

Defendant Wells Fargo Bank's motion to transfer venue is denied.

Plaintiff Darshan Kamdar was terminated from Wells Fargo in 2022 after three decades of employment. Kamdar sued Wells Fargo for violations of the Fair Employment and Housing Act ("FEHA"), as well as the Labor Code and Business & Professions Code. Wells Fargo now moves to transfer venue to Contra Costa, the county in which Kamdar worked, under the FEHA venue statute, Gov. Code 12965(c)(3).

Both parties agree the FEHA venue statute, not the general venue statute, controls. (Accord, Brown v. Superior Court (1984) 37 Cal.3d 477, 488.) Under section 12965(c)(3), a FEHA action may be brought in any county in which "[1] the unlawful practice is alleged to have been committed, [2] the records relevant to the practice are maintained and administered, or [3] the aggrieved person would have worked - but for the alleged unlawful practice." This statute is construed liberally to afford aggrieved employees a wide choice of venue. (Brown, supra, 37 Cal.3d at 486.)

Wells Fargo argues venue is improper in San Francisco on all three prongs, but its argument fails on the first. On prong one, Wells Fargo maintains the alleged unlawful conduct occurred only in Contra Costa because that is where Kamdar worked. Wells Fargo relies heavily on Malloy v. Superior Court (2022) 83 Cal.App.5th 543, which addressed whether an employee working remotely from her Los Angeles home could bring a FEHA claim against her Orange County-based employer in Los Angeles County. (Id at 547.) The court noted some unlawful employment practices were committed in Orange County, e.g., evaluations of the plaintiff's employment status and the decision to fire her. (Id. at 554.) But the alleged interference with plaintiff's right to take pregnancy disability leave occurred in Los Angeles because plaintiff resided in, and exercised her leave rights from, Los Angeles. (Id. at 555.) As unlawful conduct allegedly occurred in both counties, the plaintiff properly elected the venue of Los Angeles. (Id.)

Wells Fargo seemingly reads Malloy to hold that unlawful conduct occurs exclusively where the employee experiences it. (See Reply 9:3-6.) On the contrary, the Malloy court carefully distinguished between alleged wrongs and acknowledged some unlawful practices like the termination decision "were undoubtedly made in" and "committed in Orange County, not Los Angeles County." (Id. at 554.) Here too, Kamdar alleges unlawful employment decisions were made by management and provides evidence that responsible managing personnel worked out of the San Francisco office. (Compl. para. 21-25; Kamdar decl. para. 4-5.) Wells Fargo neither objects to nor controverts this evidence. Accordingly, Kamdar has sufficiently shown venue is proper in San Francisco on prong one of the FEHA venue statute.

Mr. Olsen is to prepare the proposed form of order.

Judge: Rochelle C. East. Clerk: Jacqueline Alameda. Court Reporter: Jaime Ply, CSR #13323, Tel. 714.316.3757, Email: jplycsr@aol.com. Deputy: Jaime. (302/RCE)
